speaker: interviewer
text: Number 5 interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder. You were describing a day when the SS came in to tell you they were leaving. I just wanted to ask you another question before you leave the ghetto-- the Warsaw ghetto. Were you aware that there had been an uprising there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not aware. We-- we heard that there were some killings there going on. And, actually, we found-- during when we worked there, we found some bodies, and as we were working with the bricks. And, yeah, we heard there was an up-- not an uprising. There were some killings there. We didn't know what's happened, how it happened, what was it. We didn't. Later, we found out some-- some about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us the-- about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as we left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember we were going through Warsaw to the city. And people, they were looking from the windows. And the SS was-- were going with us, and both sides with their weapons, their open weapons. And they were taking us through the city. And we were about 6,000 people, and lines of five people each-- each line. And we were walking very fast. We did about 40 kilometers a day, which is about 28 miles-- yeah, about 28 miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. Actually, we stopped, I think, once a day after four hours, or five hours, of walking. We stopped once a day. They did not-- they didn't give us water. And it was hot. It was in August. And I remember we were trying to pick up some water from the-- from the road, dirt, from the dirt, pick up some water and drink whatever we can from the dirt. And once we got to that Vistula River, near the river, some people were trying to get in the water to drink from the river. And they were shot. Plus, whoever couldn't walk that fast, you were shot right away. The SS, they came with motorcycles. And-- and-- and they were looking. Whoever cannot walk, he was trying to stop or something, they were shooting him. And as we were going, I was so thirsty. And I said to my father, I can't take it. I must go to the water, to the Vistula River, and try to drink there. And he said-- he didn't let me. They would have shot me. He didn't let me. He said, wait, wait, wait. And we were trying from the dirt to get a little water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, we-- we had with us a little bit. They didn't give us any food on the road. We had with us something. They give us a little bread, and margarine, and something they gave us. And I don't know if we got-- in the evening, when we arrived, we slept on fields outside. We arrived on a field. And we slept there the whole night. And I don't know if we got some soup. I don't think. Probably, we didn't get. And I remember one day, as we were walking-- oh, yes, they were in some places, some Polish woman came out with buckets of water in some areas. And they didn't let them. They shot in the air. And they didn't let them come near. And we arrived on one field. And from there, I remember a holiday. That was in a Sunday in '44, in a Sunday. It was Tisha B'Av. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Tisha B'Av?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But we knew it was Tisha B'Av. Somebody told us there, some of who kept the dates, I mean the holidays. And we arrived on that field. And we were terrible thirsty. And we tried with our-- with our hands to dig on the field, and with our soup spoon, and with our soup bowl, tried to dig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we dig, and we dig, And we found water. And all around there, every-- we all find water. And even the SS came. He said, that's a miracle. How is it possible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, we dig here three-- three meters. We can't find water. And we find that water. And the whole night, we were drinking water from that. And it was, I don't know, maybe 20-- I mean 10 inches, or 12 inches, or something like that, we found water. And I think because of that, more people escaped. And we were drinking the water there the whole night. And so every day, we were-- and then we arrived on another field that was Germany already, inside the border of Germany. And we arrived to a rail station. And there, we were sleeping on the field there. And I woke up in the morning, I was-- to water up to here. It was raining the whole night. And I didn't feel-- I was so tired and everything, was sleeping the whole night. And we were almost covered with water. And then they put us in the trains, in the cattle trains. And, again, about 100 people to a cattle train. And they gave us-- on the trains, they brought in some-- some herrings, salted herrings. And they didn't give us water. And they brought in-- once in awhile, they brought in buckets-- a bucket, one bucket with water. And naturally, everybody was-- people were getting crazy. It's because of the thirst. It was so unbearable, this thirst. I felt-- I felt also my heart was like-- like going out. And it was unbearable. Thirst is terrible. Hunger it's terrible too, but thirst is unbelievable. I never knew thirst is such. And they-- when everybody was trying to get to the buckets. So they threw out the whole water on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, I was lucky. I was in the front there, and-- because you were on one side-- the 100 people were on one side. And on the other side, the middle was the door. And you had to leave this open.

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS were coming in and out from there. And we had so many people deaths. In our wagon, there were about 30 deaths on that road.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you march?

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched about-- we did about 400 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, 400 kilometers? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably about-- no, maybe less, maybe about 320 or 10, something like that. I think we marched about six or eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware that the Russians were so close by, or what the conditions of the war was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know nothing. At that time, we just-- they took us out one day. We didn't know why or what. We have no idea what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you marched 10 people in a row, did you talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to my father. He was near me all the time. We were marching together. And we always started-- and, oh, I'm tired. I'm tired. I'm tired. No, we have to do it. We have to do it. And he was trying to-- to-- to help me, even--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Something kept me going, because I was ready there to go to the river. And they would have killed me. But I don't know. Something kept me going. And-- and-- I think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think God was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you knew it was Tisha B'Av. In this period, both in Warsaw and in Auschwitz, Birkenau Auschwitz, did you pray as a group? Did you daven daily? Did you celebrate any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I tell you, we davened one time Yom Kippur, when we were in Allach, in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I get there, I will tell you about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, otherwise, did people say prayers together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no prayer, no nothing. You couldn't do nothing there. You didn't have a prayer book. You had nothing, you know? So you didn't have nothing. You didn't have prayers. You didn't have nothing, absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone within your group, you know the smaller group that you were in, that was sort of a leader, or someone you could rely on, or someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was-- no, they just-- only the capo, which was the commander of that barrack. And there was no other-- no leaders from any leader you can rely on him or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got on the train, did you know where you were going? You knew you were in Germany, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew we were in Germany, because it was a German town. It was the town-- I don't recall the name of the town. It was a border town. And we knew we are in Germany already. We didn't know we are going to Dachau. And they put us there. And that was a very cruel, very hard train-- train walk from-- I mean drive from there to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made that one harder than?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was harder, because of the thirst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, to the herring.

speaker: interviewee
text: The herring, and the salted herring. And you couldn't-- and you were there, I think four or five days in that wagons, you know? You didn't have water to drink. And some people drank their urine, actually. And it was so bad. The people-- some people got crazy. They-- they just-- and a lot of people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when-- if a person got crazy? Did anybody come to comfort them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you couldn't. You didn't have-- nobody-- nobody-- everybody was terrible situation. They couldn't even talk to each other. It was just terrible. That was such a pain with that thirst. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never heard of Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived. And we saw a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Day time or at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day time, we arrived in the morning, I think about 10:00 or something. The train, when we arrived to the-- in the concentration camp, the railroad was there. And we went into the-- they took us in in the camps. Oh yeah, we had a lot of-- during that trip, we had a lot of-- in our heads, and, how you say, parasites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lice, oh yeah, we had lice, and lice, and lice. And-- and I never saw such big lice. You know, I never knew that they exist some type of lice like that. And we arrived to Dachau. And they took away all the clothing, everything. And, again, we went to the showers there. And they shaved us. And they gave us new clothing, the same type of pajama type, blue stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your numbers? Did they change them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I got-- I got new numbers. My numbers 90,321. My father's was 90,320. And we got new numbers. They put us in barracks there. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, cell appell in the morning, and in the evening, and during the day. And I remember, we were-- that was a nice day. It was August, middle of August, sometime end of August. It was warm. And we were there in-- there were several barracks. In between each barrack was a little yard. And we were outside there. And we were talking to each other, because we didn't work there in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were talking-- first, we were talking about-- I had some people there who were doctors. There were two doctors from Oradea, which his son was a friend of mine. He was in the same school with me at the time in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he talked to him and told him, doctor, with my body, how long can I keep it still alive? He said, OK you can probably another four weeks survive. My father, maybe you can five weeks survive. You know, we were talking about this. You know, we were so weak, and-- and we lost so much weight. Were most of the people in your barracks in Dachau from Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from Dachau in the barracks, Hungary and some other places too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you communicate with other people, in terms of language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Language, we communicated in German, if they were from other countries. They were from Greeks, like-- yeah, there were people from Greek, from Saloniki. And some were from France, some people there. And I-- yeah, I didn't tell you, we arrived about 2,600 from the 6,000, all died on the road. It was terrible. Now, when we arrived early, we were so thirsty. So they didn't give us water. So we went to the-- there, there were bathrooms, running bathrooms, like-- like in-- in the barracks there, there was a running bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we put in the soup bowl in the bathroom. And we got water. And we drank the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for days and days, our body was so deh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dehydrated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dehydrated, that for, I think two or three days, we were always taking the water from the bathroom and try to drink. And I remember one time there, they brought in some older Germans-- not Jews-- for Munich. I think-- I heard they were social-- from the Socialist Party or something. And they threw out on the window some bread, which was spoiled bread. It was all yellow, yellow and blue, spoiled complete. And they threw it in the yard, in our yard. You were crazy going after it, and grab a little bit, and eat it. We are eating it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and I said-- I said to myself, you know, we-- we pull our bread at night. You put away a little piece from that 10 grams of [INAUDIBLE] got a little bit. And always, I said, oh my God, when can I have some bread to have enough to have bread? Bread was such a big deal. And there, we were about four weeks in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't work there. We were just outside in cell appell, staying hours and hours, counting. And they took us from there to another camp, Allach.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us by-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think-- I was just trying to remember if they took by-- I think they took us by foot. Yeah, we walked, we walked. That was-- Dachau was about 10 kilometers from-- from Allach from Dachau, Allach. Allach is the town where the [INAUDIBLE] motorbike and the BMW plants are. At that time, they had their-- they-- they manufactured their airplane engines. And they took us to Allach. And there was a big concentration camp. There was a Jewish camp. We were there. And near our camp, there was another camp. There were Russian prisoners there, and some others too. They took us to that camp. And I was still with my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went out to-- we went to-- they gave us a barrack, separate beds, three beds like a bunk, you know? And with a blanket, and-- and-- and-- and I didn't have any more my boots. My boots got-- after the Warsaw march, my boots got already-- they were gone. So in Dachau, I got the wooden sandals. They didn't gave you shoes. They gave you the open wooden sandals. And that's what I wore in the cold in the winter, in the snow, and the-- whatever, wooden sandals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and in Allach, we went out to work every day in the morning. It was cell appell in 4:00 o'clock in the morning. Then at 7:00 o'clock or so, 8:00, they took us to work. And we worked-- we worked on the rail yards. The American were bombing constantly there, during the day, and the English during the night, around the camp there. And they were bombing Munich, I mean of Munich, and Allach, and all around. And we were repairing the rail stations, the railroads, and plus some walls that were destroyed there, the bricks and those kinds. We were working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sabotage in fixing rails and stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no sabotage there. There was nothing. You know, I'm not aware of anything. And we were working there. And after a-- it was very hard-- hard work. And food was very, very little. And even Sunday, they put us to work. They couldn't take us out, because they were closed everything on Sunday. But they worked us in the camp. We had to carry in our jackets-- we put the jackets inwards. And with a back to the front, we were carrying stones and dirt from one place of the camp to the other place, back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reason, just to work you, not to have any rest. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You still had the same capo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, different capo. There were different capo. And there was a Jewish capo in-- in-- in Allach. I don't recall his name, but a different capo, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people ever treated the same way, or did some have better treatment than others, worse treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were treated the same way, except if you got into some kind of position. And I was lucky. I spoke some German. And they were asking me if I speak German there. And they put me as a-- in the kitchen, as a potato peeler. We peel potatoes for the SS, because that kitchen cooked for the SS, not for our people. Our people didn't get peeled potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were working there about 15 people, peeling potatoes the whole day. That was a good job already, because I-- I was able to take a few potatoes with me for my father and a few other people. And it was very, very-- because they would have-- dangerous, you would have found, they would have shot me right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, they took me in as a-- I was washing the floors there all day. And that was a big kitchen, mopping the floor the whole day. And-- and then I was working, also, in the-- we had a warehouse there. There was a warehouse. And they sent us in to Munich to bakeries to pick up bread, and supplies for the SS. And some bread also for the-- our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent you? How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent myself, and four or five other people from-- from the camp there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go by?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a truck, and with three SS guards with the-- with the-- with the machine guns. And they took us into Munich. And there, I saw the destructions with the bombardments. It was-- we went to a bakery, it was destroyed. We went to another bakery, destroyed. So we had to go to a third bakery. And it was always something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we picked up their supplies. And we brought it back to the camp. Then I had a little better life there, because-- number one, because I worked in the kitchen, they put me in a separate barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me clean clothing every day, because we cooked for the SS too. So I have that. I was not a cook. But I have that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was able to have a separate barrack. And I was able in the evenings, when I-- before I went home to sleep. And we didn't have to go out for cell appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to us in the kitchen. They counted us there. We were in line. We were about 20 people there, and count. They counted us there. And in the evening, we went out-- when I went home to sleep, before I went to sleep, I took my father some food, which the SS leftover. So I got some better food. I took him some food. And also, I had a few people from my town which I know. I left them potatoes in different places. I had-- I hid them in different places. And they know where, because I told them where they are going to find it the next day. And-- and [INAUDIBLE], he was joking with me, one of the-- he was a doctor, Dr. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we went home, we were liberated, went home back to Romania, he was telling me, how can I pay you back the potatoes? And we were laughing always about it. And him and some other-- I had about three, four people, another one [? Grun, ?] Eugene [? Grun. ?] And he escaped too. So I was able to do that. And then I find out, my father was a-- he was a tremendous smoker. And he was smoking at home two packs a day or so. He was so addicted to smoking, in the camp, from the food what I gave him, he was giving away some of them for cigarettes, for a few for cigarettes, because they were always going down [INAUDIBLE] they were able, some people, have a cigarette or two, and buying a little food for bread or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, I find out this. And my father got-- so then I had a little-- I was a little better position, because I wouldn't have survived otherwise, if I wouldn't have been that-- because I remember I had several times diarrhea when I was working. And I was trying-- it was winter cold. I was trying to put that-- under-- under my-- my clothes, I was trying to put that blanket. If they would have find me, I would have been in big trouble. And-- and I lost a lot of weight. So I-- my father-- once I was-- you asked me if I was sick, I was sick once in-- in Allach. I got a terrible toothache, just-- just terrible. And I went to the-- we had a little, they called it, [GERMAN]. It was like a clinic hospital type, a barrack. And there, they worked three doctors-- two doctors from Oradea, from my wife's town. And we know them. I know them. And I went to him. And I said to him, look, I'm terrible [INAUDIBLE]. He said to me, you are-- my face was like this, like a stone. And he-- and he-- and he said to me, I have to open it. We don't have medicines. We don't have nothing. We don't-- we don't have-- I have a little alcohol here. I have nothing else. I'm going to open it for you from outside. And you can see it here still. He opened, and he drained the whole thing from there. And he said, I'm going to try to do it-- he was joking me, you should be able to get married. And-- and he made an opening without anaesthesia, without nothing. It was terrible pain. And-- and he took out that. And-- and I got better. And that was a time when I was actually sick. But my father got sick. He-- and-- and-- and this doctor told me, look, I must send your father back to Dachau. I said to him, no, I don't want him to send. Let him stay here. I'll be able to help him. I'm still helping him. And he-- and he was working outside. He had to go outside everyday working. He said, look, I can't. He's sick. He can't go out working.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if he's not going out, they will take him right away to the crematorium. So I said, how can you be that that selfish, not to let him go to Dachau? So I said to him, you know-- I mean-- it wasn't up to me, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: But maybe-- he said, no, no, he has to go. And so he sent him there to Dachau. And that was really unbelievable in the camp. After three days, we had-- we had a man with a wagon, one guy from Auschwitz, a wagon, horse wagon. He took every second day, the deads, from our camp to Dachau, to the crematorium. He took 30, 40 deads, 50 deads, whatever it was, every--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change our tape now again.
